data.title: Hints Of A Weak Jobs Market Ahead Of Thursday's Payrolls Report
data.description:
The government has reopened, and the task of piecing together the missing economic data points has started. Early clues suggest that hiring slowed in October. Meanwhile, the Labor Department will release its delayed payrolls report for September tomorrow.

Reviewing a bit of new continuing claims data from the Labor Department, Reuters reports: “The number of Americans on jobless benefits surged between mid-September and mid-October, government data showed on Tuesday, suggesting an elevated unemployment rate in October as an uncertain economic environment discourages hiring.”

Fed Governor Chris Waller weighed in this week and cited “eye-popping” layoffs from major companies and a slump in consumer confidence. He added:

“I am not worried about inflation accelerating or inflation expectations rising significantly. My focus is on the labor market, and after months of weakening, it is unlikely that the September jobs report later this week or any other data in the next few weeks would change my view that another cut is in order.”

The view of economists for Thursday’s release of payrolls data for September indicates that hiring picked up modestly, albeit from a sluggish level. Econoday.com’s consensus point forecast calls for a 50,000 increase in non-farm payrolls. That’s an improvement from August’s weak 22,000 increase, but not by much. If correct, the September update will align with signs of a slowing labor market in October via other sources.

ADP’s weekly employment report, for instance, shows that US companies cut 2,500 jobs per week on average in the four weeks through Nov. 1.

Revelio Labs says payrolls for October fell 9,100, according to its estimate.

Despite signs of a soft labor market, market expectations have turned cautious on the outlook for another rate cut by the Federal Reserve at next month’s policy meeting. The recent confidence that the central bank would cut for a third time on Dec. 10 has faded to a coin flip for estimating the odds, based on Fed funds futures.

Although the Fed’s Waller has been leaning in favor of additional cuts, disagreement among Fed officials has widened lately as concerns about inflation persist.

“It’s reflective of a ton of uncertainty,” said Luke Tilley, chief economist at M&T Bank. “It’s not surprising at all that there’s a wide divergence of opinions.”

Fed Chairman Powell signaled the fuzzy outlook for policy recently, noting: “A further reduction in the policy rate at the December meeting is not a foregone conclusion—far from it,” he said late last month. “Policy is not on a preset course.”

Markets will be keenly focused on Thursday’s payrolls report for deciding if Powell’s guidance is due for an update.

Original Post

data.title: NRG Energy: Buy Into A Structurally Tight Power Market And Data Center Super-Cycle
data.description:
Thesis

A company that is in the middle of a structural shift in the U.S. electricity market is NRG Energy (NYSE:NRG).

The demand is rising strongly due to electrification, the return of industrial production and the immense growth of data centers.

In this context, the company combines strong profitability, disciplined capital allocation and a clear investment strategy for growth.

Q32025 results were excellent and justify the (for the third consecutive year) guidance upgrade, with EBITDA and EPS at historically high levels.

At the same time the company is building a comprehensive portfolio of data center power, with 445 MW already contracted. The pipeline reaches 5.4 GW through the collaboration with GE Vernova and Kiewit, with prices now above $80/MWh.

The acquisition of LS Power, which is currently being finalized, critically strengthens the production base and geographic diversification.

With a CAGR of approximately 14% EPS through 2029, with no data center contribution yet, the stock offers a rare combination of visible growth, strong cash flow, and aggressive capital returns.

I've written about NRG before, and in this environment and I remain with a "BUY" recommendation.

Corporate Profile and Strategic Landscape

As I see it, NRG has evolved from a traditional producer to an integrated energy and services platform for the end consumer.

The company operates in key competitive markets with a focus on ERCOT, PJM and select East Coast states.

Electricity demand in Texas has grown nearly 30% in five years, according to administration data.

This growth is being fueled by population dynamics, electrification, industrial relocation and energy-intensive artificial intelligence applications.

Through the acquisition of LS Power and the Texas Energy Fund program, it is adding approximately 15 GW of natural gas and 7 GW of virtual power plants.

At the same time, it is developing partnerships targeting an additional 6 GW of capacity under joint development with strategic partners.

Smart Home is also a very important pillar as NRG controls one of the largest portfolios of connected homes.

Growth is strong, with double-digit profit growth and very high customer loyalty.

The home-based virtual power plant gives NRG the ability to pool flexible resources from thousands of homes.

This enhances its value in the power mechanism, reduces exposure to extreme events and improves the customer experience.

This demonstrates a sense of balance between growth and shareholder returns.

The capital return program covers buybacks of at least $1.3 billion in 2025 and a fresh authorization of up to $3 billion through 2028.

Meanwhile, aiming for 7%-9% annual dividend growth per share generates a predictable, stable stream of returns.

The company is, in a general sense, the rare look of something growing fast and still being capital disciplined.

Data Centers, BYOP and the LS Power Transformation

The main catalyst for the next five years is NRG’s role in powering the next generation of data centers.

For reasons of cost and reliability, the weight is moving toward the “Bring Your Own Power” model that a growing number of major tech companies have settled on.

And instead of depending on regulated tariffs they incur the full costs of new generation capacity to serve their own loads.

NRG is in privileged position, with available sites, commercial expertise and access to large-scale capital.

So far, 445 MW of the long-term agreements have been executed in ERCOT and PJM out to 2032. Prices have hit above $80 a MWh already, more than the administration’s earlier target range.

The pipeline of agreements under Letters of Intent and joint development with GE Vernova and Kiewit reaches 5.4 GW.

This picture shows an escalation of interest from hyperscalers and data center developers in an environment of limited new capacity.

The acquisition of LS Power complements this strategy.

The company is becoming a big, independent power producer in America with exposure to long term demand expansion.

If part of the 5.4 GW pipeline is locked in on terms similar to the existing deals, the upside margin becomes very significant.

The market, for now, seems to be conservatively pricing both LS Power’s potential and the future value of AI contracts.

Financials

Q32025 financial results show that NRG is executing strongly in every area.

Adjusted EBITDA reached historic high and adjusted EPS increased by 32% y-o-y.

In the nine month period EPS improved by 36% and Cash flow before growth investments rose than last year’s levels due to stronger profitability and working capital.

Stand-alone guidance for 2026 shows further EBITDA growth, mainly from higher prices in Texas and previous acquisitions.

Higher financial expenses and taxes are slightly limiting free cash flow growth but not stopping it.

Even without LS Power, the profitability profile remains strong, while the acquisition clearly adds additional potential.

Valuation & Forward Scenarios for 2026

NRG currently trades at around 19x earnings, a level that reflects how the market continues to view the company as a mature energy producer.

But the picture has now changed. The rapid expansion of BYOP deals, the growing momentum of data centers and the integration of LS Power are creating a new earnings profile in which the valuation multiple cannot remain at traditional levels.

In this context, the valuation for 2026 can be described through three realistic scenarios.

In the bullish scenario, EPS are $10-$11, driven by accelerated new contracts, high pricing of long-term agreements and the direct contribution of LS Power.

Such a level of earnings justifies a P/E of 24 and 26x, as the market recognizes a new growth profile that combines energy, technology and stable flows.

The corresponding share price is between $240 and $285 (up to +70%) which turns NRG into a pure AI-infrastructure growth story.

In the basic case, EPS are $8.2-$8.6 per share. This range incorporates smooth progress on BYOP projects, steady growth in retail and smooth integration of LS Power.

The P/E is 19-21x at a valuation that balances stable cash flows and increasing exposure to data centers.

The share price is $155 to $180, with slight upside from the current levels and stabilizing trends to absorb the high 2025 performance.

In the bearish scenario, EPS are $7-$7.3 per share due to delays or slower contract development.

The P/E is 15-17x which brings the stock at $105-$120 (-25%). As I see it, the downside remains manageable thanks to LS Power's stable cash flows and added value.

The overall picture shows that NRG clearly has an upward asymmetry. The bear case has limited depth while the base and bull cases offer substantial prospects for the stock to be repositioned at a higher valuation, in an environment where energy is becoming the critical pillar of artificial intelligence.

The stock is following a long-term upward trend and according to the above analysis, it has climbed from the $30 area (two years ago) to 165.

Even more interesting than the positive performance itself is the qualitative way it was recorded, with few pullbacks and controlled movement.

For the continuation, support is at 149, with the next technical target at 215 resulting from the range of the fluctuation.

Risks

As all Companies, NRG has its own risks.

The first is the the regulatory environment in ERCOT, PJM and other competitive electricity markets.

Changes in capacity mechanisms, capacity auctions or regulatory price interventions may affect future returns.

A second one is related to he execution of the data center strategy.Large-scale projects require time-consuming interconnection, licensing and construction of new units.

Delays in interconnection queues or Texas Energy Fund projects can shift revenues and pressure valuations.

In addition, hyperscalers have strong bargaining power and can change or shift their investment plans.

Third, the LS Power acquisition embodies classic integration risk.

The combination of two large production portfolios requires sound organization, integration of trading, risk management and unit maintenance.

Any delays in leveraging synergies or unexpected costs could limit the expected increase in profits.

Fourth, the company remains sensitive to interest rate movements and spreads as it finances significant investments through debt.

Refinancing old, low-interest issues with more expensive money partially reduces the benefit from new projects.

Correspondingly, negative surprises in natural gas or electricity prices could affect margins, despite hedging programs.

Finally, in the retail segment, competition remains intense, especially in states where consumers compare directly with regulated tariffs.

Further margin compression in these markets should be offset by the strengthening of the Texan portfolio and Smart Home.

Conclusion

NRG is in a rare position where long term market forces are clearly working in its favor.

Demand for power, particularly from data centers, is growing faster than supply in key competitive markets.

The company has already demonstrated that it can translate these trends into tangible financial results and rising guidance.

The addition of the LS Power portfolio, 5.4 GW pipeline and Smart Home ecosystem further strengthen the investment narrative.

Given its growth profile, strong cash flow and consistent capital return program, the stock justifies a "BUY" recommendation for investors seeking exposure to the next wave of the U.S. energy market.

data.title: Alger Concentrated Equity ETF Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • During the third quarter of 2025, the largest portfolio sector weightings were Information Technology and Consumer Discretionary. The largest sector overweight was Utilities and the largest sector underweight was Information Technology.
  • The Information Technology and Utilities sectors contributed to relative performance while Industrials and Health Care were among sectors that detracted from relative performance.

Market Environment

In the third quarter of 2025, U.S. equity markets extended their upward trajectory with the S&P 500 Index up 8.12%. Strong equity market performance was propelled by a combination of improving economic conditions, solid corporate earnings, and growing expectations for monetary easing. A key driver was the shift in Federal Reserve (Fed) policy outlook: what began as a cautious stance going into the third quarter evolved into a more dovish tone as signs of labor market softening emerged. Fed Chair Powell’s remarks at Jackson Hole reinforced this pivot, and the September 25-basis-point rate cut marked the resumption of monetary easing. While uncertainty remained about the pace of additional cuts, the prospect of lower rates supported equity valuations and investor sentiment. At the same time, inflation—though still above the Fed’s 2% target— showed signs of moderation, allowing markets to focus on the likelihood of policy support rather than restrictive measures.

Corporate earnings provided another strong tailwind, as second-quarter results broadly exceeded expectations, and third quarter guidance remained upbeat despite some tariff- related cost pressures. Many firms successfully mitigated these headwinds through pricing strategies and operational efficiencies, while consumer spending stayed firm, buoyed by steady gains in household net worth and greater clarity around trade tariffs. Within the S&P 500 Index, the Information Technology sector outperformed, driven by continued enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI), along with robust demand for cloud and semiconductor products. In contrast, the Consumer Staples sector underperformed the index. While consumer resilience supported market optimism, there was growing acknowledgment that lower-income households are under significant strain, and even higher-income families are feeling pressure, as reflected in increased ‘trade-down’ shopping behavior.

During the quarter, we continued to observe secular themes that we believe are creating attractive investment opportunities—corporations are digitizing their operations, cloud computing is growing and supporting innovation, and AI is at an inflection point, potentially enabling significant increases in productivity. Moreover, U.S. business spending appears to be accelerating, driven by a confluence of factors—most notably burgeoning demand for AI infrastructure and tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill, which allow companies to deduct 100% of eligible equipment and qualifying manufacturing-facility costs in the year placed in service, boosting near-term cash flow and after-tax returns.

Investment Strategy

Invests in a focused portfolio of 30 or fewer holdings consisting of companies of any capitalization identified through our fundamental research as demonstrating promising growth potential. Seeks long-term capital appreciation.

Portfolio Update

The Alger Concentrated Equity ETF outperformed the Russell 1000 Growth Index during the third quarter of 2025. NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) and Astera Labs, Inc. (ALAB), were among the top contributors to performance.

  • Nvidia Corporation is a leading supplier of graphics processing units (GPUS) for a variety of end markets, such as gaming, PCs, data centers, virtual reality, and high-performance computing. The company is leading in most secular growth categories in computing, and especially AI and super-computing parallel processing techniques for solving complex computational problems. In our view, Nvidia’s computational power is a critical enabler of AI and therefore essential to AI adoption. Shares of Nvidia contributed positively to performance largely because the company delivered results and guidance that reinforced its leadership in the artificial intelligence boom. The company reported record revenue and earnings that exceeded analyst estimates, driven by surging demand for its AI-focused chips and related data center products. Specifically, the strongest growth came from its data center segment, which benefited from widespread adoption of Nvidia’s advanced processors by cloud providers and enterprise customers building AI infrastructure. This momentum was supported by the ramp-up of its next-generation Blackwell chips, which are designed to handle increasingly complex AI workloads. Investors were also encouraged by management’s upbeat outlook for the coming quarter.
  • Nebius is a leading provider of AI infrastructure and cloud-based solutions, delivering specialized GPU computing capabilities designed to support large-scale AI workloads for enterprise and research clients globally. We find Nebius compelling due to its impressive growth trajectory and expanding deployment of advanced Nvidia GPU technology within data centers across Europe and North America, positioning it as a key beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption. During the period, shares contributed positively, aided by continued demand for AI compute and a landmark multi-year agreement with Microsoft. Under this deal, Nebius will supply dedicated GPU infrastructure to power Microsoft’s AI services, marking Nebius’s first long-term contract with a major cloud service provider. We believe this partnership not only validates Nebius’s technology and execution capabilities but also provides a significant revenue anchor, reinforcing its role as a critical player in the global AI infrastructure ecosystem.
  • Astera Labs designs semiconductor solutions that help large-scale computing systems—such as those used in cloud services and artificial intelligence—move data quickly and efficiently. Its products reduce “bottlenecks,” which occur when data flow slows down inside servers or between chips, limiting overall performance. The company’s Intelligent Connectivity Platform combines specialized hardware with its COSMOS software to create flexible, cost-effective solutions for customers. These technologies are critical for connecting servers, GPUs, and AI accelerators. We believe Astera is well positioned in fast-growing markets like AI and cloud computing, supported by strong partnerships with leading technology providers. Shares contributed positively during the quarter after the company reported better- than-expected fiscal second-quarter results and raised next quarter revenue guidance. Management highlighted steady progress in expanding its networking business, which enables the rapid movement of large volumes of data—a critical requirement for AI computing. Near-term growth is being driven by the company’s P-series switches, hardware devices that direct data traffic within high-speed networks. Management expressed confidence that the upcoming X-series switches, designed for even faster and more efficient data handling, may become a major revenue contributor starting in 2026.

Netflix, Inc. (NFLX), GFL Environmental Inc (GFL), and Twilio, Inc. (TWLO), were among the top detractors from performance.

  • Netflix is a global leader in streaming entertainment, delivering premium video content through a subscription-based platform that now includes an advertising-supported tier and selective live-event programming. We find Netflix to be a compelling investment for its ability to sustain strong engagement at scale, its pricing power, and its expansion into new revenue streams such as advertising and live events. In our view, management’s emphasis on consistent revenue growth and profitability, rather than headline subscriber metrics, supports a more predictable financial profile. During the quarter, shares declined as investors looked past strong fiscal second-quarter results and focused on full year guidance and second-half profitability. Management’s full-year revenue raise was attributed largely to foreign-exchange tailwinds rather than stronger underlying demand, which disappointed expectations following strong year-to-date performance. Moreover, commentary around higher content and marketing investment in the second half of 2025 tempered margin expectations, adding to investor concerns. Despite these near-term headwinds, we believe Netflix remains well positioned due to its global scale, advertising initiatives, and selective expansion into live programming.
  • GFL is a waste and environmental services company undergoing a significant transformation of its balance sheet. Over recent years, the company pursued extensive mergers and acquisitions, resulting in substantial debt. GFL has since committed to reducing this leverage by the end of 2025 through divesting non-core assets and emphasizing strong free cash flow generation. Despite delivering a solid quarter, shares detracted from performance as revenue guidance underwhelmed investors looking for faster growth. Management also highlighted plans to use proceeds from a major asset recapitalization to reduce debt and fund acquisitions, which kept attention on execution rather than near-term earnings growth. Additional acquisitions, such as Superior Waste, reinforced long-term strategy but raised integration and leverage concerns, leading to a muted market reaction despite otherwise positive fundamentals.
  • Twilio is a leading provider of Communications Platform as a Service (CPaaS), enabling enterprises to embed messaging, voice, and other real-time communication capabilities into their applications without building complex infrastructure. For example, text alerts from a bank or automated appointment reminders from a doctor’s office are often powered by Twilio. Its platform combines conversational AI, real-time analytics, and compliance features. Strategic partnerships and continued innovation reinforce Twilio’s role in this evolving ecosystem. During the quarter, shares declined despite solid second-quarter results, which included double-digit revenue growth and record free cash flow. The pullback reflected management’s outlook for slower third-quarter profit growth and gross margin pressure from a heavier mix of messaging traffic and rising carrier fees, as well as tougher year-over-year comparisons.

data.title: Cytokinetics: A Heavily Mispriced Cardiovascular Platform
data.description:
While Cytokinetics (CYTK) investors go through the ups and downs surrounding the aficamten PDUFA event, let's not forget that aficamten is only a small part of the company - the forward flank, if you will. Behind it comes a concerted strategy to make Cytokinetics the first emerging platform company in precision cardiac mechanics. All of this is based on myosin modulation biology, so we will discuss a bit of that. We will also discuss the economics of cardiac diseases, which resembles oncology in some respects - prescriber concentration, biomarker driven, expanding population - but which also differs in two fundamental respects - one, more extensive chronic disease prevalence in cardiology, and two, a less differentiated market.

Aficamten is proof of the platform

The latest corporate deck makes it quite clear that management views aficamten in oHCM as merely the first proof of the platform, not the entire business. The deck makes it clear that they have one platform, four drugs in various clinical stages, four large cardiovascular and one neuromuscular segment, and 10 New Molecular Entities (NMEs) in their pipeline. Their cardio franchise consists of oHCM, nHCM, HFpEF and severe HFrEF, all built on their myosin modulation platform.

This is reminiscent of how Regeneron (REGN) built an entire business under the VEGF biology; with the key difference that unlike in ophthalmic diseases or cancer, CYTK's muscle mechanics field has almost no competition.

There are scientific and commercial reasons to choose oHCM as the first platform validating program. oHCM is genetically very well defined, since it is driven by specific mutations in sarcomere proteins (e.g., MYH7, MYBPC3). This is also directly linked to the myosin pathway. Moreover, the pathology - hypercontractility - is consistent, and there are not a variegated set of unrelated mechanisms here. oHCM causes measurable, defined physiological changes - like Left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) gradient, for example. LVOT, as well as Peak VO₂ and KCCQ, are clean endpoints accepted by regulators. Finally, per the company's earnings call, approximately ~650 HCPs constitute 80% of the entire HCM prescriber base, making commercialization easier. So once the company can prove that myosin modulation = improved gradients = improved symptoms in a safe and tolerable manner, the same biology can be ported to adjacent phenotypes. Which is something CYTK is already doing with its nHCM program (ACACIA). The HFpEF and severe HFrEF programs, both historically graveyards for multiple therapies, could also see traction if CYTK can use myosin platform to adequately control myocardial contractility.

So let's not look at aficamten's approval as just the opening up of a revenue stream. That is there, but its more important function is to derisk the entire cardiomyopathy platform. Once that is done, CYTK can presumably leverage the same small HCP base for most of the other indications. That repeatability of effort modules - well-known in technology sectors - makes this approval all the more important.

Unusually High Revenue-per-Rep Curve

CYTK's ability to precisely control cardiac contractility - up or down - is a sparsely populated field. CYTK has spent two decades here developing its expertise. This matters because historical failures in HFpEF and severe HFrEF came from drugs that hit broad pathways but produced off-target hemodynamic effects.

The real edge in Myosin biology is its ability to directly control sarcomere function. Here, the same platform can generate activators (omecamtiv), inhibitors (aficamten, ulacamten) and variants tailored to specific phenotypes. This single discovery engine producing molecules for multiple large indications while being able to use the same commercial channel is CYTK's distinguishing factor. Once aficamten is approved, we will already know that the biology works (trial data already tells us so) and is approvable. As CYTK moves on to nHCM, HFpEF, and severe HFrEF, the next challenge will be simpler - not if the biology works, but how to adapt the contractility modulation for each indication. The commercial channel will have already been perfected.

As I pointed out earlier, there are many similarities between cardiology targeted by CYTK and oncology, in both structure and economics. I already noted the heavy concentration of prescribers in the HCM indication. The same specialists will treat the 250-400K nHCM patients, ~840K severe HFrEF patients (EF <30% by 2029), and 800K HFpEF patients with EF ≥65% by 2030. The scale of this population resembles oncology - large, growing, and served by a small number of specialists. A small team of 100-150 high quality reps can effectively saturate the entire market within a matter of weeks. Similar to oncology, this structure produces high revenue-per-rep.

Aficamten's Label May Be More Disruptive Than Modeled

Analysts will model aficamten as a second entrant in the CMI (cardiac myosin inhibitor) category, where Bristol Myers Squibb's (BMY) mavacamten is the first entrant - however, what we hear from the regulators (via CYTK management) is that the label may be far more disruptive than assumed. Mavacamten may have proved that CMIs work, but aficamten almost every operational, pharmacologic and clinical metric. Mavacamten has a long half-life and a rigid REMS, which has resulted in slow real-world adoption. Aficamten, management tells us, will have a very differentiated REMS and label, and its much rapid onset and action, flexible titration, much stronger data, makes it a much more practical drug.

If this works out, the distinction plays out in the real world, not on paper. Mavacamten's REMS creates a bottleneck - it limits flexibility through echo-based monitoring, it limits who can prescribe, and slows titration. The contrast becomes clearer in the MAPLE-HCM data published in NEJM in May. Moreover, mavacamten's failure in ODYSSEY-HCM in nHCM brings out this contrast given aficamten's positive data in the nHCM cohort in REDWOOD and FOREST. If ACACIA-HCM, with dual primaries that mavacamten could not meet, becomes successful (trial is fully enrolled, topline data will be out in Q2'26), aficamten will no longer play second fiddle to mavacamten. Already aficamten's superiority to beta blockers, improvement in exercise capacity, KCCQ, LVOT - and singular safety advantage with very low and reversible incidence of LVEF <50% - indicates a less restrictive REMS. This opens two doors.

First, it expands the prescriber base. Many centers may well have found adopting mavacamten difficult because of all the rigidities I mentioned here. They may well find aficamten easier to use. This is most especially true of non-academic cardiologists who may not have instant access to echo appointments, staff dedicated to monitoring drug-specific REMS, or capacity to manage titration, repeat imaging and so on, as specified by the mavacamten REMS. For them, a lighter REMS and less restrictive echo monitoring will allow them to prescribe aficamten easily.

This also leads to the second "door," that an easier REMS accelerates switching. Patients using mavacamten - and HCPs prescribing it - will be intimately aware of the monitoring difficulties, so a differentiated aficamten label will immediately appeal to them.

The sell-side is still treating aficamten as a me-too drug, but that may well change if REMS and label become meaningfully differentiated. The company commentary strongly hints so. Aficamten will become the go-to molecule in oHCM among CMIs, and once that happens, CYTK's broad market in parallel phenotypes opens up wide. The large market here is nHCM, where ODYSSEY-HCM led to a bearish consensus for the entire CMI class in nHCM. Given the data I referenced - and assuming ACACIA is successful - the valuation gap will become even more apparent.

Beyond HCM, real long-term expansion comes from HFpEF and severe HFrEF, whose combined population size dwarfs HCM by a factor of 2-3x. Not only is this market larger, it is also harder, where mavacamten cannot follow, and where CYTK's two decades of data generation gives its differentiated myosin modulation platform mechanistic relevance. By 2028, HFpEF and severe HFrEF could well become the core focus of CYTK - none of which are reflected in today's valuation.

Launch Is Not Capital-Constrained

So does CYTK have the balance sheet strength to launch aficamten while advancing ACACIA, omecamtiv and ulacamten, all without further dilution? Between the last $650 million convertible raise, the Royalty Pharma financing structure, and careful balance sheet management, CYTK now has a clear cash runway that comfortably covers all these programs. Importantly, the Royalty Pharma deal leaves the near-term HCM franchise largely untouched, allowing it to benefit from this upcoming revenue stream.

The aficamten launch should not need a massive footprint, and like we have seen, it will be reusable as well. The SG&A ramp should be modest compared to traditional drug launches. According to their latest earnings call, the company has $1.25 billion cash, cash eq and investments as on September 30, 2025. On top of that, they received $100 million from the Tranche 5 Royalty Pharma loan in October 2025 and has access to another $175 million via Tranche 7 upon aficamten's first approval. There may be other investments totalling $325 million after this.

This is particularly important because the company guided towards $680-700 million in operating expenses for 2025, which already includes some commercialization costs. With $1.25 billion in hand and another $325 million more available, the company is funded through aficamten launch, ACACIA readout as well as through ongoing development of ulacamten (HFpEF) and omecamtiv mecarbil (severe HFrEF).

Valuation

There appears to be a severe valuation gap in how little of the platform is actually reflected in the stock. Although aficamten is weeks away from approval (PDUFA Dec 26, 2025), and despite the myosin mechanism data we have so far, and despite CYTK owning the potential market, the market continues to look at CYTK as a one-product cardiology company. Here I discuss a proper SOTP DCF valuation that paints a very different picture.

We start with obstructive HCM, where a conservative estimate of the treatable population across the US and EU is around 80,000-100,000 patients. If we price aficamten at around $110k-$130k, take a high prescriber concentration, and assume an easy REMS, we can model for a 35% market share in oHCM by year 7. Applying an 11% WACC, 1.5% terminal growth, and a 90% probability of success, we get peak sales at around $2.2 billion and the present value of aficamten in oHCM around $7.7 billion.

The mispricing becomes starker in nonobstructive HCM. Since mavacamten's failure in ODYSSEY, the Street continues to extrapolate that to the entire CMI class in nHCM. Now, the treatable nHCM population is at least 250,000-400,000 patients, with ~200,000 in the symptomatic NYHA II-III subgroup that would map to ACACIA. Using $120k annual net price, peak penetration of only 10%, we get a global opportunity of $1.7 billion. Applying a 55% probability discount, the net present value in nHCM becomes $3.4 billion, which is a figure largely absent from consensus models.

HFpEF and severe HFrEF are the two largest long-term opportunities, and both are heavily discounted in current models. There are well over 1.6 million US patients, and very few programs have ever worked. Even if I assign a 5% penetration in HFpEF and 7% in severe HFrEF, at a lower net price of ~$90k, the combined peak revenue exceeds $8 billion globally. Applying only a 20% probability of success, this risk-adjusted net present value adds another $4 billion. Current models ascribe no value to these programs despite they being fully-funded and in late stages, with the proven biology blended in.

Taken together, the SOTP valuation we get becomes hard to ignore. Combining all four indications and cash, we get a combined equity value of $16.5 billion, which, on a diluted share count of 122 million, gives me a $135/share fair value. Even removing all heart failure programs and looking at CYTK as a pure HCM play, we get a fair value of $90, nearly 40% above current price ($66). And if ACACIA succeeds, the removal of the probability discount leads to a fair value of $158.

Risks

Cytokinetics carries several meaningful risks. The most immediate is the regulatory uncertainty surrounding the initial label and REMS of aficamten. Despite management's hopeful note, this needs to be more flexible than mavacamten before the entire story can take off. Any surprise here will disrupt the valuation model above.

The second risk is ACACIA's failure to validate aficamten over mavacamten in nHCM. That may not invalidate the biology, but may delay the entire process while CYTK continues to be considered as a one-pony show. The heart failure programs are even riskier (which is why we gave a much lower probability of success).

Third, while we have focused on the concentrated HCP base, commercial execution risk still exists. This is CYTK's first commercialization, and missteps, pricing, payer negotiations, etc. could hurt topline. And, even with a lighter REMS, switching may not be instantaneous given many HCPs will have become invested in mavacamten.

Fourth, although CYTK's debt is mostly long term and due to deferred revenue accounting, it is still substantial at over $1 billion, and carries revenue-sharing obligations with Royalty Pharma. This would meaningfully reduce free cash flow in some years, and although valuation does have a cushion, the spread may be stressed.

Finally, while competition risk is low, it is not zero. BMY is a huge competitor, and new agents targeting sarcomeric mechanisms could emerge in the next few years. Other approaches, like gene-therapies or device-based ones (common in cardiology), could proffer competition. Investors need to be aware of these non-trivial risks before considering investment in CYTK.

Bottom Line

As we move towards the PDUFA, Cytokinetics moves away from being a speculative story to becoming an emerging leader in precision cardiac mechanics. They will have a validated platform, a leading CMI class medicine, and clearer visibility in larger indications. Aficamten's approval is a major derisking event for the company, unlocking nHCM, HFpEF, and severe HFrEF, each of which is materially larger than obstructive HCM. If there's a differentiated REMS and label, and with the stronger data, safety and concentrated prescriber base, aficamten has several advantages working in its favor. Add to that the formidable cash moat for an emerging biopharma, and the valuation discussion above which pegs a fair value of CYTK at anywhere between $90 (ultraconservative) and $158 (moderately optimistic), and we have to say that CYTK is a compelling BUY.

data.title: Market Crash Revisited: Buy Recession-Resistant Income Now
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

The strength in the stock market continues to be unconstrained. Investors are buying hand over fist – the indices remain near all-time highs, and valuations aren't at all-time highs, but are close to it.

Today, we are seeing something that defies common sense. The stock market believes that the S&P 500 is going to see earnings growth over the next five years that is higher than any earnings growth ever achieved over a 5-year period.

At the same time, consumers are as bearish on their personal economics as they have ever been.

Wall Street thinks everything is wonderful, while Main Street is worried. They can't both be right.

The Oracle of Omaha

On February 28th, 2001, Warren Buffett wrote his annual letter to shareholders. Note that this was early on in the Dot-com bust, and before the recession truly set in. He noted what he saw as Wall Street overly celebrating recent triumphs, writing: Source

"Now, speculation - in which the focus is not on what an asset will produce but rather on what the next fellow will pay for it - is neither illegal, immoral nor un-American. But it is not a game in which Charlie and I wish to play. We bring nothing to the party, so why should we expect to take anything home?

The line separating investment and speculation, which is never bright and clear, becomes blurred still further when most market participants have recently enjoyed triumphs. Nothing sedates rationality like large doses of effortless money. After a heady experience of that kind, normally sensible people drift into behavior akin to that of Cinderella at the ball. They know that overstaying the festivities – that is, continuing to speculate in companies that have gigantic valuations relative to the cash they are likely to generate in the future – will eventually bring on pumpkins and mice. But they nevertheless hate to miss a single minute of what is one helluva party."

In context, 1999 was the worst year for Berkshire Hathaway in history relative to the stock market. Over the prior two years, the S&P 500 and BRK arrived at more or less the same place through very different paths:

He went on to discuss the unrealistic expectations that Wall Street had on future growth:

"Last year, we commented on the exuberance – and, yes, it was irrational – that prevailed, noting that investor expectations had grown to be several multiples of probable returns. One piece of evidence came from a Paine Webber-Gallup survey of investors conducted in December 1999, in which the participants were asked their opinion about the annual returns investors could expect to realize over the decade ahead. Their answers averaged 19%. That, for sure, was an irrational expectation: For American business as a whole, there couldn’t possibly be enough birds in the 2009 bush to deliver such a return."

That's right, investors seriously thought in 1999 that earnings were going to grow at 19% for 10 years. Wow, people sure were crazy 25 years ago! Can you believe that anyone would seriously believe that is possible?

What The Street Believes Today

Well, before we laugh at the delusions of those who came before us, let's take a look at what we think today: Source

S&P 500 LTEG (Long-Term Earnings Growth) 17.6%.

Normal is 10-12%. Now we know COVID threw a monkey wrench into things, causing a collapse in earnings that artificially drove very high earnings growth numbers in 2021 relative to those during the shutdown in 2020 as companies reopened. Today, companies have stabilized from that disruption, and Wall Street believes that they will grow at 17.7% average over the next five years.

That's an irrational belief.

What About Consumers?

The interesting thing is that Wall Street's outlook isn't consistent. It believes that the S&P 500 will see rapid growth, primarily in AI. This is similar to the Dot-com outlook on the Internet.

Yet, if we look at something like Consumer Discretionary, the earnings growth outlook is outright bearish: Source

Wall Street is bearish on earnings growth in most sectors, with only tech and healthcare being standouts. In other words, Wall Street believes that consumers are going to be doing horribly and aren't going to be a great source of earnings.

This actually aligns well with consumer sentiment being near its lowest ever, while in 2000 it was near its highest ever:

My question is, if all this earnings growth isn't coming from consumers – where is the money coming from?

The Value of AI

The value of AI isn't AI for the sake of AI. Sure, people have fun chatting on ChatGPT about random things, but nobody is going to pay $200/month to use ChatGPT for entertainment purposes.

The value proposition of AI is very similar to the value proposition of the Internet. The Internet transformed the economy because it changed how we do economic transactions that we were already doing. The early adopters of the Internet had an advantage, but that advantage has declined as using the Internet is now just a normal tool that everyone has. Companies that had a technological edge quickly lost it and had to go on to compete on the more mundane factors like price, quality, and customer satisfaction.

AI is changing the world. I think, at this point, there is very little question about that. However, the barriers to adopting AI are a lot lower than they were for the Internet. In the early days of the Internet, there were real physical barriers. Infrastructure across the country needed to be upgraded before consumers could access the Internet at reasonable speeds.

AI adoption from the consumer standpoint has no barriers. I don't have to get a new satellite dish or have someone come dig up my yard to install a cable to upgrade my service. If I want to use AI, there's a whole bunch of programs I can quickly access from my computer or my phone. I can easily swap from ChatGPT to Gemini; I can access either through a 3rd-party wrapper, and it's all available right there on the Internet that I already have. As a consumer, I'm simply paying for the result, and for any task I can imagine, there are already multiple competitors.

Moving from one AI ecosystem to another is as easy as cancelling one and starting the other, and it takes just 60 seconds.

As a result, the first mover advantage is unclear and might be nonexistent. The field is already super competitive, and it's only going to get more competitive. In a market economy, when competition is high, prices and profits decline.

Action Opportunities

Where can we get dividends from these dynamics?

Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG), yielding 6.5% is a CEF (Closed-End Fund) that invests in utilities and infrastructure. Whatever happens with the economy, AI is going to continue to increase demand for energy of all types. UTG is an excellent fund with a history of dividend increases and of navigating economic turmoil, such as the GFC.

Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY), yielding 13%, is a mortgage REIT that invests in agency MBS, a countercyclical asset that tends to do best when Treasury prices are strong – that means periods of economic uncertainty. It isn't a coincidence that NLY saw high prices and rising dividends during the Dot-com bust and the GFC. From December 2000 to December 2002, NLY saw its dividend increase by 170%. From December 2007 to December 2009, it saw its dividend rise by 210%. It is one of the few business models where a recession is great for business.

Fixed-Income: When the economy falters, interest rates decline because the Fed cuts rates to stimulate the economy. We are in a period right now where interest rates are high relative to where they've been in several decades. They've started coming down, but are still high relative to what we have seen in the past 20 years. We've been buying preferred equity and bonds to lock in these high yields. While prices will be volatile in any market collapse, having a stream of cash flow coming from a robust fixed-income portfolio is a huge advantage.

Conclusion

Don't get me wrong, there will be winners to emerge from the AI arms race. However, I view the rapid ramp-up in AI CapEx as major companies struggling to keep up with everyone else. They aren't racing to secure new future profits; they are racing to ensure that they can keep the profits they already have before consumers leave their ecosystem for another one. It is a race to survive, not a race to thrive.

The real winners from the Internet boom were not the companies that developed the Internet. They were the companies that found creative ways to make the Internet better for consumers. AI will be no different. The real gains will be from the companies that can find a way to make AI as useful as possible to the end consumers at a low price.

The S&P 500 isn't going to see earnings growth at +17% over the next five years – Wall Street believing that, is the greatest indicator that we are in a bubble. Participating in bubbles is fun, and if you want to put out some gambling money and speculate, have a great time. But don't make the mistake that so many did in 1999. Don't take for granted that Wall Street is right about earnings growth and that nosebleed valuations will remain forever. They never do.

Fortunately, there are a lot of sectors where growth expectations are realistic and maybe even conservative. There are a lot of opportunities in the market that are trading at valuations that are much closer to recession lows than bull market highs.

So, while many are running around buying companies that are trading at +20x P/E Ratios, about 10% of companies are trading at P/E ratios below 10x: Source

We target investments that are trading at a P/E Ratio of 8-12x, because that's where you are going to find companies producing high enough earnings relative to their stock price to pay high yields.

The most common sense investment advice ever offered is "buy low, sell high", yet so many in the market will tell you that a stock that is trading at a high valuation is lower risk because it is expensive. That is an assertion that defies common sense, and you should trust your common sense. Sometimes, an expensive investment is a good one; often, it's just expensive.

Stay focused on buying investments that you believe are trading at an attractive price, will have attractive cash flow, and will provide you with dividends based on their earnings today. Don't count on +17% earnings growth to justify your investments, because it probably isn't happening.

data.title: Bitcoin: Crypto Winter? A Contrary View
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate the recommendation to buy Bitcoin (BTC-USD) even after the recent price drop. This article is the continuation of my initial coverage thesis published on November 29, 2024, and my last article on the topic was published on October 3, 2025.

Bitcoin fell below $90,000 for the first time in seven months, and many investors are beginning to panic, especially as several analysts argue that they may be at the beginning of crypto winter. My intention with this article is to refute such arguments , and reaffirm the investment thesis in Bitcoin remains attractive.

First Argument

In my opinion, we need to revisit basic investment principles in this case. The Fear & Greed Index indicates that Bitcoin investors are scared right now. However, as Warren Buffett would say, you should be cautious when others are greedy, and greedy when others are cautious.

If we use this concept, would you prefer to buy Bitcoin when it is at an all-time high, or when it is at $90,000? Note that in the first case, the risk-return ratio is unattractive, however, we have a great chance of obtaining good returns in the second case, and this is my first reason for you to be greedy at this moment.

In parallel, although I am not a technical analysis enthusiast, the chart below shows that Bitcoin's relative strength index is at extremely low levels, and at other times, this was a great buying opportunity.

Second Argument

The investor needs to remember the reasons that made him invest when the price is not in his favor. In the case of my investment theses, the arguments were: Crypto-Friendly Politicians , investment flow to ETFs , institutional and corporate adoption. If you look closely, all the pillars are still preserved.

A great recent example was Citi announcing that it should launch cryptocurrency and Bitcoin custody services in 2026. In short, don't let yourself be manipulated just by the price! Of course you should observe, but also observe the reasons that led you to buy Bitcoin.

Third Argument

After analyzing basic investment principles, I intend to analyze technical data now. The graph below shows that the amount of bitcoins available on exchanges is decreasing radically. But what is the interpretation of this? Basically, the immediate supply of bitcoins for sale is reduced, which tends to be positive, not to mention that such a movement preceded strong increases in the past.

Fourth Argument

Bloomberg Intelligence reported the outflow of $2.7 billion in Bitcoin ETFs in the last month. While many may feel that this undermines one of the pillars of the investment thesis, we can see from the chart below that the movement is not linear. This is only about 1.5% of total assets. In my opinion, this is a market movement rather than a sign that one of the pillars of the investment thesis has weakened.

Fifth Argument

Finally, I will provide a comparison that will also serve as a metric to estimate a target price. According to the study below, if there is a rotation of just a small percentage of gold for Bitcoin, we are talking about an unimaginable jump in prices.

If we are conservative and estimate a rotation of just 2%, we have an upside of 50%, which would take Bitcoin to a price of $140 thousand points. Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy Bitcoin. But of course, you need a trigger for this.

I believe this upside could come from President Trump's idea of ​​giving checks to the population for the gains from tariffs. In 2020 when the US Government sent checks, Bitcoin soared by more than 350%. Now, let's analyze the risks to the thesis.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

I will recommend that investors be aware of some risks. The first is the sales volume of bitcoin holders . For reference, at the end of September, around 815 thousand bitcoins had been sold by these holders , one of the highest levels since January 2024.

Another signal to monitor comes from technical analysis. Although I am not an enthusiast, apparently Bitcoin has lost the 50-period simple moving average, which has been acting as a support in recent times.

Finally, it is always interesting to pay extra attention when an asset in your portfolio appears in headlines like the one in the figure below. When brokers, banks and other market participants begin to raise target prices for an asset, it is a sign of skepticism.

The Bottom Line

Bitcoin has reached $90,000 again, and of course, we have many more worried investors than investors ready to take advantage of the opportunity. Likewise, many analysts are looking for reasons to corroborate the thesis of Crypto Winter.

I disagree with these pessimistic opinions, and brought robust arguments to indicate that we are facing an opportunity. Basically, all the arguments that point to rising Bitcoin prices remain intact, and what we are contemplating in my opinion is a market movement.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy Bitcoin. In my opinion, situations like we currently see were great opportunities in the past, and I believe they will become opportunities again.

data.title: Beware Of The Risk Of The 7.4% Preferred Yield Of Seritage Growth Properties
data.description:
After having raised interest rates to 23-year high levels, the Fed recently changed its policy and is now in the process of reducing interest rates in order to provide a boost to the economy and reduce the unemployment rate. As a result, the time is ideal for income-oriented investors to lock in some attractive yields from fixed-income securities before these yields decrease amid declining interest rates.

The preferred stock of Seritage Growth Properties (SRG.PR.A) is currently offering a 7.4% dividend yield, which is safe in the absence of a default of the REIT. A fixed income stream of 7.4% is certainly attractive for income-oriented investors, particularly if the Fed keeps cutting interest rates as per its guidance. Nevertheless, before buying a preferred stock, investors should examine two factors: the safety of the dividend and the outlook of interest rates. In this article, we will evaluate the safety of the 7.4% preferred dividend of Seritage Growth Properties and analyze whether there is a risk of higher interest rates in the future.

Safety of the dividend

The preferred stock of Seritage Growth Properties offers a quarterly dividend of $0.4375. This dividend corresponds to an annualized dividend yield of 7.0% when the preferred stock trades at its par value of $25. However, the preferred stock is currently trading at a 5.2% discount to its par value, and hence it is offering a 7.4% dividend yield. Just like most preferred stocks, the preferred stock of Seritage Growth Properties is perpetual (no expiration date) and cumulative, i.e., any forgone dividends will be paid whenever the company has sufficient liquidity to distribute them.

In addition, the preferred stock of Seritage Growth Properties is callable at its par value of $25. As the stock is trading at a 5.2% discount to its par value, the preferred shareholders will benefit if the stock is called. However, Seritage Growth Properties is facing liquidity issues. Consequently, the preferred stock is unlikely to be called in the short run.

Seritage Growth Properties is a REIT that owns, develops, and operates retail and mixed-use properties in the U.S. Unfortunately, the REIT is under liquidity pressure. As a result, it is in the process of selling assets in order to strengthen its balance sheet. As per its latest earnings report, it has interest only in 13 properties. On the bright side, Seritage Growth Properties has sold most of its assets at prices close to their book value on average.

On the other hand, there are some red flags, which should not be undermined by investors. First of all, Seritage Growth Properties has been posting material losses every single year over the last decade. Notably, the loss per share of -$1.42 in the last 12 months is equal to 40% of the market capitalization of the stock. Even if the interest expense and the tax expense are excluded, the company has been posting material operating losses every year over the last 9 years.

It is also worth noting that depreciation costs have greatly decreased in recent years, from $226.7 million in 2018 to $8.1 million in the last 12 months. On the one hand, this is natural, as the REIT has been selling assets without investing in new ones. On the other hand, the fact that the REIT keeps posting material losses even amid depressed depreciation costs raises a red flag for the future liquidity of the REIT.

Seritage Growth Properties has net debt (as per Buffett’s formula: Net debt = total liabilities – cash – receivables) standing at $161 million. This amount is 77% of the market capitalization of the stock, and hence it appears reasonable, particularly if the company keeps selling its assets at prices close to their book value. The latter is now standing at $6.00 per share, or $338 million. Therefore, if Seritage Growth Properties continues to sell its properties at prices around their book value, it is likely to be able to pay off its net debt and redeem its preferred shares, which have a nominal value of $70 million.

In its most recent earnings report, management stated there are 4 properties under contract for estimated gross proceeds of $241 million. The company is also negotiating the sale of its interest in 3 joint ventures for gross proceeds of approximately $47 million. The expected gross proceeds from the sale of the 7 properties, which amount to ~$288 million, will probably be sufficient to cover the payment of net debt and the preferred shares (161+70 = $231 million). If the proceeds of the remaining 6 properties are added in the picture, it seems that the REIT will be able to pay off its debt and its preferred shareholders. As a side note, the company has not provided an estimate for the gross proceeds of these 6 properties.

On the other hand, management recently emphasized that it is facing challenging business conditions, such as elevated interest rates. While the Fed has executed a few interest rate cuts, interest rates remain elevated compared to their level before 2022. As a result, raising equity capital for land development deals remains hard. Management stated that the current business conditions may exert downward pressure on the sale prices of the remaining assets.

To conclude regarding the safety of the preferred dividend, the REIT is likely to manage to pay off its debt and keep paying its preferred dividends under normal conditions. The book value of its properties is sufficient to provide the required liquidity for these obligations. However, if a severe downturn shows up, such a recession, the REIT may be forced to sell its assets at depressed prices. Overall, the base scenario is positive for the preferred shareholders but the preferred dividend is not entirely safe.

Interest rates

Due to the surge of inflation to a 40-year high of 9.1% in mid-2022, the Fed raised interest rates aggressively in 2022 and 2023, to 23-year highs. However, a few months ago, the central bank changed its policy and prioritized the reduction of unemployment over the reduction of inflation. As a result, it has cut interest rates twice this year and has provided guidance for further interest rate cuts next year.

Lower interest rates provide a tailwind to preferred stocks, as they render the dividends of the preferred stocks more attractive. This helps explain the 8% gain of the preferred stock of Seritage Growth Properties in the last 12 months. However, due to the impact of tariffs on the prices of consumer goods and services, inflation has rebounded from 2.3% in April to 3.0% in September.

Even worse, the effect of the recent interest rate cuts has not shown up on inflation yet, as it takes many months for this effect to become visible. Lower interest rates boost the economy by increasing the total amount of investments but it also causes inflation to rise due to a heated economy. Therefore, there is a risk that inflation rises significantly next year. In such a case, the Fed may shift its stance once again and may raise interest rates. Such a development will probably exert pressure on the preferred stock of Seritage Growth Properties. It will also exert pressure on the asset sale process of the company, as per the aforementioned statement of management.

Overall, as per the guidance of the Fed, interest rates are not likely to increase anytime soon. This is positive for Seritage Growth Properties, both for the sale of its assets and its preferred stock price. On the other hand, the risk of a resurgence of inflation and possibly higher interest rates in the future cannot be excluded.

The bottom line

Under normal business conditions, Seritage Growth Properties is likely to complete its divestment project successfully and thus the 7.4% preferred yield of the stock will reward the preferred shareholders. However, the above-average preferred yield is not risk-free. Management has already put emphasis on the challenging business conditions prevailing right now. If the company faces an unexpected downturn, such as a recession or a resurgence of inflation and interest rates, it may face liquidity issues. This is the reason behind the superior yield of the preferred stock of Seritage Growth Properties vs. the preferred yields of other REITs. To provide a perspective, the preferred stock of Public Storage (PSA.PR.F) is offering an essentially safe 6.0% dividend yield. Overall, the preferred stock of Seritage Growth Properties appears unsuitable for risk-averse investors.

(a) Liquidation Preference is $50 per share.

Fund Performance

The S&P 500 Utilities Index rose 17.7%, outperforming the S&P 500 Composite’s 14.8% return. The utility sector significantly outperformed early in the year amid recession and tariff concerns before investor appetite shifted toward growth.

In the U.S., the utility investment case is strengthening as electricity demand grows at its fastest pace since the mid-20th century, driven by AI-powered data centers, reshoring of manufacturing, and electrification of transportation. Utilities are responding with record capital investment in generation and grid upgrades, often in partnership with hyperscalers like Amazon, Microsoft, and Google. Policymakers remain broadly supportive, creating a favorable backdrop for sustained rate base and earnings growth. Importantly, rising demand allows infrastructure costs to be spread over more kilowatt-hours, helping manage bill impacts. Still, execution risk remains as utilities must finance, build, and manage large-scale infrastructure efficiently. Federal policy momentum also supports the sector, with efforts to streamline nuclear permitting, expand gas-fired capacity, extend coal plant lives, and reduce regulatory bottlenecks.

In Europe, the focus remains on the long-term clean energy transformation, the need for adding scalable renewables, and resilient grids to meet Europe’s decarbonization targets. Year to date in 2025, European wholesale power prices are higher than 2024 prices, primarily driven by rising European gas and global LNG prices. Gas-fired power generation sets the marginal price in competitive power markets, including those in Europe. In addition, Germany plans to add roughly 20 GWs of gas on its goal to 80% renewable by 2030. Electric demand growth is more modest than in the U.S., and investment is driven by Europe’s industrial electrification, net-zero goals, and grid investment. European utilities have increased a focus on networks (grid) investment given the intermittency of renewables and grid vulnerabilities underscored by a major blackout in Iberia in April 2025.

Lower interest rates offer an added tailwind: the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield fell to 4.16% from 4.58% at 2024 year-end, and markets expect multiple Fed cuts through 2026. Lower yields support utility valuations and reduce financing costs, while utilities’ 3.5% median dividend yield and 9%–11% total return potential enhance their appeal.

Our outlook remains constructive, with prospects for stronger EPS growth, easing macro pressures, and rising infrastructure asset values driving M&A interest.

data.title: Blackstone Secured Lending: Strong Quarter, But Don't Get Too Excited Yet
data.description:
Introduction

If you followed me on here, then it's likely you know I'm an income-focused investor and have been bullish on BDCs (BIZD) my entire time here.

After selling out all of my positions earlier this year due to suspecting headwinds from declining interest rates, I've been waiting for an opportunity to get back in.

While BDCs have seen declining share prices due to lower base rates, some still trade at premiums above their net asset values. But with interest rates expected to continue declining in the coming months, I think investors may get even better entry points.

Me, personally, I'm looking for some BDCs for an all-income portfolio I'm building, and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) is a contender. BXSL recently reported a strong Q3, with their bottom line increasing from the prior quarter.

While this strengthens their dividend profile temporarily, investors should be wary of further headwinds in the near term.

In this article, I discuss BXSL's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why investors shouldn't get too excited just yet.

Previous Hold Thesis

I last covered Blackstone Secured Lending back in August in an article titled: I Say Chances Of A Dividend Cut Are About 50/50.

During their Q2, BXSL saw their dividend coverage fall to 100% as their bottom line decreased $0.06 due to increased competition, macro uncertainty, lower investment commitments, and lower base rates.

Their top line also retracted quarter-over-quarter but still managed to increase 5.4% from the prior year. Despite this, BXSL's fundamentals remained strong, evidenced by their low non-accruals, manageable leverage, and robust liquidity.

Due to a high potential to see further rate cuts, I suspected BXSL could potentially reduce its dividend, resulting in further downside. Since, the stock is down close to 12% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), up over 4%.

Best Quarter Since Q4'24

Blackstone Secured Lending reported their Q3 earnings and saw their share price rally as a result. This was due to the BDC seeing their strongest quarter since Q4'24.

Deployments in the quarter crossed $1 billion due to increased investment activity, a 90% increase from the previous quarter. And resulted in a 15% year-over-year increase in the portfolio to $13.8 billion.

This added 22 borrowers while exiting six, bringing their total borrower count to 311 from 252 a year ago.

Blackstone Secured Lending's quarter was rather impressive, seeing how net investment income managed to increase despite lower base rates causing their portfolio yield to decline 20 basis points from Q2. This fell 120 basis points from 11.2% in the prior year's quarter, an explanation for the declining dividend coverage.

NII grew from $0.77 to $0.82, giving BXSL solid dividend coverage of 106%. However, this was still down from $0.91 the prior year's quarter, a result of declining interest rates. Total investment income climbed from $345 million to $359 million, and grew 4.7% year-over-year.

Strong Credit Quality

BXSL's latest quarter further showed why they're one of the most fundamentally-sound BDCs in the sector. Most of their commitments in Q3 were weighted towards first-lien loans, bringing their total exposure to 97.5%. This was slightly down from 98.7% last year's quarter.

Still, for comparison purposes, BXSL still had one of the most defensively-positioned portfolios amongst its peers:

Ares Capital (ARCC): 61%

Hercules Capital (HTGC): 90.5%

Capital Southwest (CSWC): 89.7%

This is also likely the reason for BXSL's low non-performing borrowers. Non-accruals were 0.1% on a cost and fair value basis, one of the lowest in the sector. This was even lower than HTGC's, whose non-accruals were 1.2% at cost and 1.1% at fair value.

Despite the strong quarter, Blackstone Secured Lending did see some markdowns, which resulted in $0.16 in unrealized losses.

This also impacted NAV, resulting in a decline from $27.33 the prior quarter to $27.15. On an annual basis their decline was slightly lower, from $27.27. This is also likely the reason for the six investment exits in Q3.

Balance Sheet Position

From a balance sheet standpoint, Blackstone Secured Lending maintains a solid position with investment-grade credit ratings from all three major agencies. Liquidity was robust with more than $2 billion available, and leverage was slightly above the peer average at 1.22x.

This increased from the prior quarter and year, but should be of no concern for investors, as BXSL just recorded their highest investment activity since last year.

However, going forward, investors should keep an eye on further increases, particularly if the economy sees further weakening, as this could indicate deteriorating credit quality.

Why Investors Shouldn't Get Too Excited Yet

Despite BXSL's strong fundamentals and the surge in investment activity, macro uncertainty still keeps me cautious.

And this is evidenced by the uptick in job losses. As a result of this and the potential to see delayed impacts from the recent government shutdown, we could see further economic weakening.

Moreover, this would also increase the likelihood of seeing more rate cuts. If so, this will result in lower yields on investments and, ultimately, in tighter dividend coverage.

I've been adamant about BDCs potentially seeing more dividend cuts in the coming months and think the sector may see further downside by Q1'26. My cautiousness on BXSL has less to do with their fundamentals and more to do with macro uncertainty and management's willingness to reduce the dividend.

Many BDC management teams have touched on potential cuts. Going forward, I, personally, think we could see more cuts in the next 3 to 6 months. While this would likely cause further share price weakness, this would still give BDCs attractive high starting yields.

Currently, the market is pricing in lower rate cut odds, down from 65% previously. But most still expect an additional rate cut next month. As a result, BDCs will see lower portfolio yields. Particularly BXSL, since they have 98.6% exposure to floating-rate debt.

This is significantly higher than ARCC's 71% and higher than CSWC's 96.3% & HTGC's 97.8%. Meaning BXSL's bottom line is more sensitive to interest rate cuts.

Going forward, investment activity could continue to increase. Especially, if the Fed can manage a soft landing. But I suggest waiting to add to any BDC, at least until the first quarter, to see how further rate cuts shake out.

Slight Discount To NAV Is Attractive

Currently, Blackstone Secured Lending trades at a slight discount to NAV with a P/NAV ratio of 0.99x. Income-oriented investors may find this attractive. Especially, since the stock is down more than 17% year-to-date.

And some peers, like ARCC, CSWC, & HTGC, all still trade at premiums above their NAVs. But the potential to see further share price weakness in the sector due to rate cuts is likely in the next 3 to 6 months.

Bottom Line

If you're an income-focused investor, adding to BXSL at a slight discount to NAV is not bad. Especially, if you plan to hold long-term. However, with economic uncertainty remaining high and the likelihood of seeing further volatility in the sector due to more rate cuts, I suggest waiting, as there could be more share price weakness. Therefore, I'm reiterating my hold for Blackstone Secured Lending.
